fact: September 13 is exactly 256 days into the year - that's 2 to the 8th power, making it a favorite day for computer programmers who love binary numbers!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime spider season! House spiders are frantically searching for mates and cozy winter hideouts, which is why you suddenly see them everywhere.
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets due west around this time, as Earth prepares for the autumn equinox in just over a week.
Maple trees in cooler regions start their color-changing magic right around now, with sugar rushing down from leaves to roots like nature's own candy factory shutting down for winter.
Why do September nights suddenly feel so crisp? The angle of sunlight hitting Earth has weakened enough that nighttime cooling happens much faster than in summer.
Monarch butterflies are in full migration mode, with some flying over 50 miles per day on their epic journey to Mexico - they might be passing right over your head!
This date falls smack in the middle of Virgo season, when the constellation Virgo dominates the evening sky and appears brightest to stargazers.
Squirrels enter their hyperactive hoarding phase around September 13, burying up to 25 nuts per day and remembering thousands of hiding spots with their incredible spatial memory.
In the Southern Hemisphere, spring is bursting everywhere - wombats are emerging from winter dens, and baby kangaroos are peeking out of pouches for the first time.
The moon appears to rise about 50 minutes later each night around this time, creating dramatically different moonrise times compared to summer when the delay was much shorter.
September 13 marks the peak of cricket chirping season in temperate zones - you can actually calculate the temperature by counting their chirps and doing simple math!
Deciduous trees are secretly breaking down the green chlorophyll in their leaves right now, revealing hidden yellow and orange pigments that were there all summer long.
